video_title: Scaling Laws: Explain to Shane (Tews) Cross-post
gist: A crossover between the Scaling Laws and Explain to Shane podcasts where host Kevin and Shane Tews dissect the Trump administration's June 2, 2026 AI executive order on pre-deployment cyber testing (cut from 90 to 30 days under reported pressure from David Sacks), the strained capacity of CISA/NSA to administer it, and the downstream workforce, education, and AI-access bifurcation issues raised by frontier AI capabilities.

insights.claim: President Trump signed an executive order on AI on June 2, 2026 that establishes a voluntary pre-deployment testing framework for highly capable models alongside cyber resilience mechanisms.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Sets the regulatory baseline frontier AI labs must navigate before releasing new models in the US.
insights.quote: on June 2nd, lo and behold, Uh, we had a new executive order get signed, which reduced that voluntary period for testing of highly capable models to 30 days

insights.claim: The voluntary pre-deployment testing period for frontier AI models was cut from an initially proposed 90 days to 30 days, reportedly after David Sacks, the former AI czar, warned Trump that a longer period would set the US back versus China.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Signals the administration prioritizes speed and US-China AI competition over extended government safety review, favoring faster model releases.
insights.quote: Folks are saying that it was David Sacks, the former AI czar, who was particularly influential, caught the ear of President Trump and said, "Don't move forward with this executive order."

insights.claim: A 90-day model testing window is effectively obsolete in AI because roughly six different models were released within a single 90-day span over the past year, per Abundance Institute's Neil Chilson.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Quantifies how the AI release cadence outpaces traditional regulatory review timelines, supporting the case for lighter-touch testing.
insights.quote: over the span of 190 day period within the the last year or so, I think six different models got released

insights.claim: The executive order leans on CISA's authority to issue binding operational directives requiring federal agencies to patch systems and adopt cyber best practices.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Identifies CISA as the operational backbone of the order, making its capacity a key dependency for implementation.

insights.claim: The June executive order failed to specify whether the 30-day voluntary testing window means calendar days or business days, leaving a material ambiguity for labs planning model releases.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Drafting gaps like this create legal uncertainty that frontier lab counsel must resolve before relying on the framework.
insights.quote: it doesn't specify whether companies are uh expected for this voluntary framework to allow for 30 calendar days of testing or 30 business days of testing

insights.claim: The 'mythos moment'—when highly capable AI models including ChatGPT 5.5 demonstrated the ability to identify and exploit software vulnerabilities—was the catalyst that pushed the White House to prioritize AI cyber resilience policy.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Pinpoints offensive cyber capability as the specific risk vector driving AI regulation, a watch-item for critical-infrastructure and security investments.
insights.quote: we had highly capable AI models with respect to identifying and exploiting software vulnerabilities. We saw it first with mythos, and then we saw it with ChatGPT 5.5

insights.claim: CISA has been hollowed out by workforce reductions over the past year and lacks congressional reauthorization, leaving it underfunded just as the executive order assigns it new AI cyber responsibilities.
insights.speaker: Shane Tews
insights.so_what: A capacity-resource mismatch that could cause the executive order's cyber provisions to underperform regardless of design.
insights.quote: an organization that has been kind of hollowed out in the last year and needing to make sure that we have the right expertise that is in place

insights.claim: The NSA was tasked with defining what counts as a 'qualifying covered model' under the voluntary framework through a classified process, drawing criticism that the determination belongs at a more AI-expert body like CAISI (Center for AI Standards and Innovation) or NIST.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Where the covered-model line is drawn determines which labs fall under federal review, and classifying it reduces transparency for the industry.
insights.quote: the NSA is the one tasked with coming up what is a qualifying covered model for this potential voluntary framework

insights.claim: The 'voluntary' AI testing framework risks becoming coercive because future administrations could publicly praise participating companies and call out non-participants, creating business and political pressure to comply.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Reputational leverage could turn a nominally optional regime into a de facto mandate, a regulatory-risk overhang for AI companies.
insights.quote: this quote-unquote voluntary framework is quickly going to become something much more coercive

insights.claim: Compute or FLOPs thresholds are becoming an unreliable proxy for AI model risk because highly capable open-source models are not lagging far behind the frontier, undermining capability-based gating definitions.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Regulators losing a clean technical threshold means scope of any AI rules will be contested and harder to enforce on open models.
insights.quote: as open-source models become, uh, highly capable themselves and aren't lagging too far behind the frontier, how do we begin to change that definition of what is truly risky

insights.claim: Cyber attack information sharing is structurally limited because disclosing vulnerabilities or attacks broadly can cause harm beyond the original victim, and DHS provides indemnification to enable companies to share critical-infrastructure cybersecurity information.
insights.speaker: Shane Tews
insights.so_what: Explains why legal indemnification (and CISA's role) is essential to private-sector cyber cooperation, a policy lever to watch.

insights.claim: Large non-tech companies running legacy equipment are highly exposed to AI-enabled cyber threats because the technology underpinning their operations creates vulnerabilities they fail to recognize or patch.
insights.speaker: Shane Tews
insights.so_what: Flags legacy-heavy incumbents across sectors as a hidden cyber-risk concentration as AI lowers the bar for exploiting old systems.
insights.quote: they they need to understand that they are making themselves exceptionally vulnerable by not paying attention to this conversation

themes.theme: AI haves vs have-nots and proprietary legal AI

insights.claim: Kirkland and Ellis is developing its own proprietary AI tool, illustrating an emerging 'tokenocracy' in which well-resourced firms gain an AI advantage that smaller competitors lacking budget and token access cannot match.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Points to an AI-driven competitive moat forming in professional services that could bifurcate the economy into AI haves and have-nots.
insights.quote: Kirkland and Ellis is developing their own proprietary AI tool

insights.claim: In trust-based professions like law, medicine, and mental health, expected AI disruption will be muted because clients pay for trusted institutions and people they trust, and proprietary in-house AI will reinforce rather than erode incumbent advantage.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Counters the AI-disruption thesis for relational industries, suggesting trusted incumbents may capture rather than lose value from AI.
insights.quote: the sort of massive disruption that people expect in some domains just won't occur because trust is still going to be something that people really want to pay for

insights.claim: Hallucination is the central obstacle for open-source legal AI, and closed proprietary models claim to have largely fixed it, creating a quality moat that protects firms from court sanctions over inaccurate filings.
insights.speaker: Shane Tews
insights.so_what: Reliability, not just access, is the differentiator in regulated-profession AI, favoring vendors that can demonstrably control hallucination.
insights.quote: their biggest problem is hallucinations and if they can find out whatever it is that's the secret sauce because a lot of these closed loop are saying that they've fixed that

insights.claim: A law firm trained AI tools to replicate how partners give feedback to junior associates, and partners report the AI-generated feedback is more or less what they would have provided in person.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a concrete path to automating professional mentorship and training, a productivity and headcount signal for services firms.

themes.theme: AI workforce transition and retraining incentives

insights.claim: The average job tenure of a Gen Z worker in the first five years of their career is about 1.1 years, which removes employers' incentive to invest 3-6 months training young hires on AI skills.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Short tenure breaks the economics of corporate AI upskilling, a structural gap that policy or new financing models must close.
insights.quote: the average job tenure of a Gen Zer within the first five years of their career is 1.1 years

insights.claim: The federal cap on training expenses companies can be reimbursed for is roughly $5,000 per employee per year, which Kevin argues is too low to incentivize meaningful AI reskilling.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: A specific tax lever; raising it could shift corporate training spend and is a concrete policy proposal to watch.
insights.quote: The cap on the amount of funds that companies can be reimbursed for when they provide training is quite low. I think it's $5,000 per employee per year.

insights.claim: The AEI Workforce for Futures initiative, co-chaired by former Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo and former House Speaker Paul Ryan, was set to be officially announced in mid-June 2026 to address the AI workforce transition.
insights.speaker: Shane Tews
insights.so_what: A bipartisan, high-profile policy effort whose recommendations could shape future AI workforce regulation and funding.

insights.claim: Workforce policy bodies are still relying on unemployment records as their primary metric for AI labor disruption, which Shane Tews criticizes as a lagging indicator inadequate for the pace of AI change.
insights.speaker: Shane Tews
insights.so_what: Reliance on lagging data means policymakers may consistently react late to AI-driven labor shifts.
insights.quote: they'd say, "Well, unemployment records." And I was like, "Wow, that is such a lagging indicator."

insights.claim: Corporate AI tools are routinely underutilized, with employees often using only about a tenth of the capability of expensive software because companies adopt tools without training staff on them.
insights.speaker: Shane Tews
insights.so_what: Highlights a large untapped ROI gap in enterprise AI spend, favoring vendors and services that drive adoption and training.
insights.quote: they use like maybe a tenth of what they could be doing with that pretty relatively expensive asset

themes.theme: Reimagining training, national service, and AI literacy

insights.claim: AI is automating the entry-level repetitive tasks that traditionally built junior professionals' skills, creating a need to reinvent how early-career experience and 'muscle memory' are developed, with virtual reality and AI simulation emerging as a way to replace resource-intensive training such as mock oral arguments.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host) and Shane Tews
insights.so_what: If apprenticeship rungs disappear, firms and schools must build new training pathways, an opening for VR/simulation edtech.

insights.claim: Kevin proposes a 'Prompt for America' national service program where AI-savvy volunteers staff local public libraries to teach teachers and small business owners how to use AI, and plans to pilot it in Austin.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: A grassroots AI-adoption model; broad literacy efforts could accelerate mainstream AI uptake and demand.
insights.quote: That's why I'm a advocate in particular for a version of national service that I'm calling a prompt for America.

themes.theme: Constitutional AI, data centers, and global regulation

insights.claim: Constitutional AI—training models on a set of values and principles so their character adheres to those values—raises the unresolved governance question of who decides which values get instilled, currently a handful of people at the respective labs.
insights.speaker: guest, Kevin (Scaling Laws host)
insights.so_what: Public participation in AI value-setting may be necessary for broad adoption; a governance/regulatory frontier for labs.
insights.quote: who gets to determine what those values are that get instilled within these models

insights.claim: Shane Tews argues there is insufficient data to conclude that AI data centers consume 'too much' energy and water, comparing the concern to golf courses' water use and likening current data-center buildout to the 1990s telecom overbuild that enabled today's internet.
insights.speaker: Shane Tews
insights.so_what: A contrarian bull case for data-center buildout framing energy concerns as overstated, relevant to infrastructure and utility investing.
insights.quote: I'm not sure what too much is because I'm not sure what was okay before

insights.claim: Global AI regulation to watch includes the EU's Digital Markets Act, Digital Services Act, and EU AI Act, plus emulation of those frameworks emerging in Japan, Korea, and Australia.
insights.speaker: Shane Tews
insights.so_what: Maps the international regulatory landscape that will shape compliance burdens for global AI and tech firms.
